skill.name: llm-application-dev-ai-assistant
skill.description: Guides development of AI assistants and chatbots, including architecture, dialog flows, context handling, testing, deployment, and improvement loops.
skill.summary: Build production-oriented AI assistants with structured architecture, conversation design, testing, and deployment guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the command and weak-crypto alerts are markdown fences or ordinary prose/code identifiers. The skill is a documentation playbook with sample Python, Docker, and Kubernetes snippets; no malicious execution, exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found. Low residual risk remains because examples discuss LLM function calling and conversation metadata collection without full production controls.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to markdown code fence delimiters in SKILL.md and resources/implementation-playbook.md. They are documentation boundaries, not Ruby backtick execution or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged location is a markdown fence opener or closer around examples. I found no evidence that these backticks execute commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts are caused by words and identifiers in assistant-design prose and examples, such as design and dialog terminology. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or encryption implementation was found at the flagged locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed surrounding lines show headings, method names, and dictionary keys about assistant design. The flagged text is not cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Dictionary Access
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts point to conversation-flow and context-management examples reading user input or next-node fields. They do not collect host, process, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines handle dialog state inside sample application logic. I found no evidence of operating-system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Deployment Example Binds to All Interfaces
low_findings.description: The Docker command binds the example service to 0.0.0.0:8080. This is common for containers, but production deployments should pair it with network policy, authentication, and ingress controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded address is present in a Docker deployment example. It is not exfiltration, but it can expose a service if deployed without controls.

low_findings.title: LLM Function Calling Requires Execution Controls
low_findings.description: The function-calling example executes registered functions after argument validation. This is legitimate architecture guidance, but real implementations need allowlists, authorization, audit logs, and side-effect limits.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example limits calls to registered functions and validates arguments, which lowers risk. It still describes executing functions selected from an LLM response.

low_findings.title: Conversation Metadata Collection Needs Privacy Controls
low_findings.description: The improvement example stores feedback and conversation metadata. This is useful analytics guidance, but implementations should define consent, retention, minimization, and redaction rules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample explicitly stores conversation metadata. The file does not show secret exfiltration, but privacy safeguards are not described in this snippet.

dangerous_patterns.title: LLM-Selected Function Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes a function-calling pattern where a function name from an LLM response is looked up and executed. This pattern is acceptable only with strict registration, validation, authorization, and logging.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code sample validates arguments and checks registration, but it still models LLM-directed function execution. The risk depends on how developers implement the registered functions.

content.value_statement: AI assistant projects need clear architecture, reliable dialog behavior, and safe integration patterns. This skill provides structured guidance for designing, testing, deploying, and improving chatbot systems.
actual_capabilities: Outlines AI assistant architecture components, including NLU, dialog management, response generation, context, and integrations.
Provides conversation-flow patterns for greetings, task requests, clarifications, confirmations, and fallback handling.
Shows implementation patterns for LLM provider integration, function calling, and response generation.
Includes testing guidance for conversation quality, edge cases, adversarial inputs, and regression coverage.
Covers deployment, scaling, monitoring, analytics, feedback collection, and continuous improvement workflows.
limitations: It provides guidance and illustrative examples, not a complete runnable assistant framework.
Example code uses placeholder classes and methods that require application-specific implementation.
Security, privacy, and compliance controls must be adapted to each production environment.
Model selection, hosting costs, and vendor-specific API details are outside its direct scope.

use_cases.title: Plan a Customer Support Chatbot
use_cases.description: Design intents, context handling, escalation flows, response rules, and quality tests for a support assistant.

use_cases.title: Implement LLM Assistant Architecture
use_cases.description: Convert high-level assistant requirements into components, integration boundaries, testing strategy, and deployment steps.

use_cases.title: Review Assistant Reliability
use_cases.description: Assess fallback behavior, adversarial cases, monitoring metrics, and feedback loops before production release.

prompt_templates.title: Define Assistant Goals
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define the goals, users, primary tasks, and success metrics for an AI assistant in [domain].

prompt_templates.title: Design Conversation Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design conversation flows for [assistant type], including greeting, task handling, clarification, confirmation, fallback, and escalation paths.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Technical Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an implementation architecture for an AI assistant with context management, LLM integration, function calling, testing, monitoring, and deployment concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this AI assistant plan for reliability, privacy, security, observability, scaling, failure modes, and continuous improvement gaps.

output_examples.input: Design an assistant for a SaaS support team.
output: Architecture overview with NLU, dialog manager, response generator, context store, integrations, and analytics.
Conversation paths for account questions, troubleshooting, escalation, and unresolved requests.
Testing checklist covering intent accuracy, fallback quality, edge cases, and handoff behavior.
Production-readiness review across safety, privacy, observability, reliability, and deployment controls.
Priority fixes for weak fallback handling, missing retention rules, incomplete test coverage, and unclear escalation paths.

best_practices: Start with user tasks, escalation paths, and measurable success criteria before choosing model or infrastructure details.
Treat function calling, memory, and integrations as permissioned capabilities with validation, logging, and rollback paths.
Test conversations with realistic, ambiguous, adversarial, and incomplete inputs before production release.
anti_patterns: Building a chatbot around generic prompts without defined intents, responsibilities, or failure handling.
Letting LLM responses trigger tools or actions without authorization, validation, and audit trails.
Collecting conversation data without consent, retention limits, redaction rules, or privacy review.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete chatbot application?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture, design, testing, and deployment guidance with illustrative examples.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this skill specific to one LLM provider?
faq.answer: No. It discusses provider integration patterns and includes examples for OpenAI, Anthropic, and local models.

faq.question: Can it help with production readiness?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers testing, monitoring, deployment, scaling, analytics, and improvement workflows.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It mentions validation and testing patterns, but production security controls need additional review.

faq.question: What should teams prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Prepare target users, core tasks, integration needs, data handling rules, and success metrics.
